manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
Provide one single-word clue that relates to some of your team's words from the list below. You can choose to how many and to which words your clue relates to.
The goal is to have your partner guess all of your team's words based on the clues you give them, before the other team has uncovered all of theirs. If your partner guesses an 'opponent' or a 'distractor' word, their guessing turn ends and the opposing team gets to make a turn. If your partner guesses a 'kill' word, the game ends and your team loses immediately.
The more words your clue relates to, the quicker you can win the game, but the harder the associations may be to guess for your partner, so choose your clue word wisely. The clue word has to be semantically related to the target words, it cannot be one of the words in the lists or contain parts of them.

Always give your single-word clue and your comma-separated list of related target words in the following format and make your answers as short as possible, never include any other text than is required in this form:

CLUE: <WORD>
TARGETS: <TARGETS>

Your team words are: tick, card, tube, eye, bed, shakespeare, belt, park, lap.
Your opponent's team words are: poison, cotton, air, dress, wake, apple, hotel, olive.
Distractor words are: well, amazon, fork.
The kill words are: sink, grace, spine, seal, mercury.

message.content:
CLUE: London
TARGETS: shakespeare, tube, park

We need to generate a single-word clue that relates to some of our team's words from the list: tick, card, tube, eye, bed, shakespeare, belt, park, lap. Provide a clue, then list of target words (the ones from our team that are related). The clue must be semantically related. The clue must not be one of the words in lists or contain parts.

We have to think about possible associations among the team words. Let's list them: tick (like a tick (insect), tick (sound), tick (mark), tick (clock)?). Card (playing card, credit card, card (paper), business card). Tube (like a tube (pipe), tube (subway), tube (television?), tube (test tube)). Eye (organ). Bed (furniture). Shakespeare (author, playwright). Belt (clothing accessory). Park (public park). Lap (thigh, lap (as in lap of a race track), lap (the act of licking?), also "lap" as in lapdog? Not sure.)

We need to find a single-word clue that is semantically related to some subset (one or more) of these team words, but not too obvious to allow partner to guess correctly. And also we need to be careful about opponent words or distractors. The clue word must not be any of the listed words or contain parts of them. Also not a kill word. The kill words: sink, grace, spine, seal, mercury. Must avoid those.

We should choose a clue that relates to multiple team words, but not to opponent's or distractor words (unless we want to purposely avoid association). Or "play" for Shakespeare, tick (play as in tick-tock?), maybe not.

Let's think of categories: "time" relates to tick (time), bed (maybe bedtime?), belt (time belt?), no. "game" relates to card (card game), lap (lap of a race?), but not.

Could choose "stage": Shakespeare wrote stage plays. Card? Not. "Stage" also is something like "stage of a race"? "Lap"? Yes, lap can be a stage of a race, as a "lap" is a stage in a race. Also "belt"? Stage of a belt? No. "park"? Stage in a park? Not.

Consider "track": "belt" maybe "belt" is like a track? Not.

Maybe "road": "park" (park a car), "belt" (belt road?), "lap" (lap of road), not.

Better think of word associations: Let's categorize each team word with a particular attribute:

- tick: insect (like a tick)
- card: something you can play (playing card), or you can write on (greeting card)

- tube (pipe) -> "pipe"

- bed (sleep) -> "sleep"
- shakespeare (theater) -> "theatre"
- belt (clothing) -> "cloth"

"theatre": Shakespeare is theatre; "park" can have open-air theatre? Not typical.

But we might also want to avoid linking to distractors: well, amazon, fork. And not link to kill words: sink, grace, spine, seal, mercury. So the clue cannot be "well" (kill? no), "amazon", "fork". Also kill words can't be used.

Thus we need a clue that is not ambiguous to lead them to guess opponent words.

Let's think of categories: we have "belt", "park", "lap". There's a phrase "belt park lap"? Not.

"Belt" and "park": "beltway" or "parkway"? That's a type of road. "Parkway" is a road, but we could think of "beltway" as a highway encircling a city, also "parkway" is a road. But "track" is also "track and field". But "belt" not.

"Belt", "park", "lap" could be associated with "area"? Not.

Maybe "circuit": A circuit can be a racing circuit (lap), a belt is a "circuit"? No.

Alternatively "circuit" can be an electrical circuit (tube?). Not.

Let's think of each team word and see if we can find a larger group.

We have: tick, card, tube, eye, bed, shakespeare, belt, park, lap.

Potential group 1: "game": card (card game), lap (a lap in a race is like a game?), Shakespeare (game?), park (play in a park?), belt? Not.

Potential group 2: "time": tick (time), bed (bedtime), maybe Shakespeare (time?), "lap" maybe "lap time" (time in a lap). Also "tube" can be "time tube"? Not.

But "time" is a good group: tick (time), lap (lap time), bed (bedtime?), Shakespeare (the time?), but "tube" maybe not. "Card" no.

But we could choose "time" and target tick, lap, maybe bed? Also "belt" maybe "belt"? Not.

Actually "belt" can be "belt" as in "belt of time"? Not.

We could pick "time". But the clue "time" is quite generic. Could partner guess other words? Let's see: if we give clue "time", we list targets: tick, lap, maybe bed? But what else? Possibly "Shakespeare"? He wrote about time (sonnets). But that's too ambiguous.

Given the rule: we give one single-word clue that relates to some of the team's words. We can pick any number (including 1) of target words, presumably all those we think are related. The goal is to get them to guess all of our team's words. So we could just give separate clues for each set and gradually reveal. But we need to eventually guess all words. Ideally we want to group them to win quicker. But we can also go incremental.

Given it's a single turn (presumably we need to produce one clue now). The problem statement: "Provide one single-word clue that relates to some of your team's words from the list below. You can choose to how many and to which words your clue relates to." So we just need to produce one clue for this turn. Probably we can produce something that covers multiple words.

We need to consider that our partner will be trying to guess which words we have, and they may guess incorrectly. They can guess multiple words? Actually they can guess a list of words in each turn? In this case, it's similar: they guess multiple words in their turn, and if they guess an opponent word or distractor, the turn ends and the other team gets to guess. Also we must ensure that any kill words are not related to the clue, because they would be dangerous; partner could guess kill words maybe if they are strongly associated. So avoid any strong link to kill words: sink, grace, spine, seal, mercury.

Thus we need to find a clue that is related to at least some of our team words, but not to any of opponent's words, distractors, or kill words. And must not be any of those words. We need to think about possible categories. Let's map all words with categories:

manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
Provide one single-word clue that relates to some of your team's words from the list below. You can choose to how many and to which words your clue relates to.
The goal is to have your partner guess all of your team's words based on the clues you give them, before the other team has uncovered all of theirs. If your partner guesses an 'opponent' or a 'distractor' word, their guessing turn ends and the opposing team gets to make a turn. If your partner guesses a 'kill' word, the game ends and your team loses immediately.
The more words your clue relates to, the quicker you can win the game, but the harder the associations may be to guess for your partner, so choose your clue word wisely. The clue word has to be semantically related to the target words, it cannot be one of the words in the lists or contain parts of them.

Always give your single-word clue and your comma-separated list of related target words in the following format and make your answers as short as possible, never include any other text than is required in this form:

CLUE: <WORD>
TARGETS: <TARGETS>

Your team words are: tick, card, tube, eye, bed, shakespeare, belt, park, lap.
Your opponent's team words are: poison, cotton, air, dress, wake, apple, hotel, olive.
Distractor words are: well, amazon, fork.
The kill words are: sink, grace, spine, seal, mercury.

CLUE: London
TARGETS: shakespeare, tube, park

The words tube, park, eye were guessed correctly. Your teammate's turn ended there.
Now provide another clue relating to some of your remaining team words and a list of the related target words. Remember to start your clue with 'CLUE: ', put a new line, and start your comma-separated list of target words with 'TARGETS: '. Notice: some words have been removed from the lists compared to previous requests.

Your remaining team words are: tick, card, bed, shakespeare, belt, lap.
Remaining words for your opponent are: poison, cotton, air, dress, wake, apple, hotel, olive.
Remaining distractor words are: well, amazon, fork.
Remaining kill words are: sink, grace, spine, seal, mercury.
